title: Capital Without Category
text:
Welcome
This website presents our project on governance, inequality, and the micro-enterprise paradox in Brazil.
Use the sidebar to navigate through our EDA, written analysis, and appendix materials.

title: Member 1 Paxton Boyd
text: Data and Setup

title: Member 1 Paxton Boyd
text:
Packages


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)          # bootstrap Gini confidence intervals
library(quantreg)      # quantile regression
library(broom)         # tidy model outputs
library(gt)            # publication-quality tables
library(modelsummary)  # regression summary tables
library(ggtext)        # markdown / HTML in ggplot labels
library(glue)          # string interpolation

# ── Shared palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653")
pal_gov  <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#e9c46a",
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#4a90d9",
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#264653"
)

caption_base <- "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry ·He, Miao & Boyd"




Data Ingestion and Cleaning


Code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Core recoding ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
co <- companies |>
  # Drop records with missing or non-positive capital (not usable for log-scale analysis)
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    capital_stock > 0,
    !is.na(owner_qualification)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Size tier (ordered factor)
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    # Governance archetype: collapse owner_qualification into 4 classes
    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    # Log10 capital for distributional analysis
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Legal-form shorthand for micro analysis
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Prop.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    ),

    # Capital-rich flag: top quartile *within* micro-enterprises
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Final observations:", scales::comma(nrow(co)), "\n")


Final observations: 141,332 




Descriptive Summary Table


Code
co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    med_cap  = median(capital_stock),
    mean_cap = mean(capital_stock),
    p25_cap  = quantile(capital_stock, 0.25),
    p75_cap  = quantile(capital_stock, 0.75),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt(groupname_col = "size") |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Capital Stock by Firm Size and Governance Archetype**"),
    subtitle = "All values in BRL · Median, mean, and interquartile range"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    governance = "Governance Archetype",
    n          = "N",
    med_cap    = "Median",
    mean_cap   = "Mean",
    p25_cap    = "P25",
    p75_cap    = "P75"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = c(med_cap, mean_cap, p25_cap, p75_cap),
    suffixing = TRUE, decimals = 1
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size            = px(13),
    heading.align              = "left",
    row_group.background.color = "#f4f4f4"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(md("*Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry*"))

title: Member 1 Paxton Boyd
text:
Analysis I — Within-Tier Inequality

Lorenz Curves with Bootstrapped Gini Coefficients
A Gini coefficient point estimate is not sufficient for inference: it has no standard error as typically presented. We bootstrap 999 resamples per size tier to construct 95% percentile confidence intervals, confirming whether the ordering across tiers is statistically reliable.


Code
# ── Gini helper ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
gini <- function(x) {
  x <- sort(x[x > 0 & !is.na(x)])
  n <- length(x)
  if (n < 2L) return(NA_real_)
  2 * sum(seq_len(n) * x) / (n * sum(x)) - (n + 1L) / n
}

gini_stat <- function(data, i) gini(data[i])

set.seed(112)
gini_ci <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  group_modify(~ {
    b  <- boot(.x$capital_stock, gini_stat, R = 999)
    ci <- boot.ci(b, type = "perc", conf = 0.95)
    tibble(
      g     = b$t0,
      lower = ci$percent[4L],
      upper = ci$percent[5L],
      n     = nrow(.x)
    )
  }) |>
  ungroup()

gini_ci |>
  mutate(across(c(g, lower, upper), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Bootstrapped Gini Coefficients by Firm Size**"),
    subtitle = "999 resamples · 95% percentile confidence interval"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    size  = "Tier", g = "Gini",
    lower = "CI Lower", upper = "CI Upper", n = "N"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0)








Bootstrapped Gini Coefficients by Firm Size


999 resamples · 95% percentile confidence interval


Tier
Gini
CI Lower
CI Upper
N




Micro
0.9858
0.9412
0.9941
66,202


Small
0.9954
0.9944
0.9962
32,610


Medium/Large
0.9967
0.9953
0.9973
42,520









Code
lorenz_data <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  arrange(capital_stock, .by_group = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    cum_firms   = row_number() / n(),
    cum_capital = cumsum(capital_stock) / sum(capital_stock)
  ) |>
  ungroup()

gini_labels <- gini_ci |>
  mutate(
    label = glue("Gini = {round(g, 3)}\n[{round(lower, 3)}, {round(upper, 3)}]"),
    x = 0.20,
    y = c(0.70, 0.59, 0.48)
  )

ggplot(lorenz_data, aes(x = cum_firms, y = cum_capital, colour = size)) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1,
              linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.55) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data    = filter(lorenz_data, size == "Micro"),
    aes(ymin = cum_capital, ymax = cum_firms),
    fill    = "#e76f51", alpha = 0.07, colour = NA
  ) +
  geom_label(
    data        = gini_labels,
    aes(x = x, y = y, label = label, colour = size),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    fill        = "white", label.size = 0.25,
    fontface    = "bold", size = 3.3, lineheight = 1.2
  ) +
  annotate("text", x = 0.59, y = 0.48,
           label = "Perfect equality", colour = "grey55",
           size = 3.2, angle = 42) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_size, name = "Firm size") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(),
                     name   = "Cumulative share of firms") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(),
                     name   = "Cumulative share of capital") +
  labs(
    title   = "Micro-enterprises are internally the most unequal — **Gini > 0.60**",
    subtitle = glue(
      "Lorenz curves by firm size tier. Labels show Gini point estimate and ",
      "bootstrapped 95% CI (999 resamples).\n",
      "The further the curve bows below the diagonal, the more concentrated ",
      "capital is among a small fraction of firms within that tier."
    ),
    caption = caption_base
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_markdown(face = "bold", size = 14, colour = "#264653"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.5,
                                    lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 14)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position  = "top",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )









The bootstrapped confidence intervals confirm that the Gini point estimates are precise: the non-overlapping 95% CIs establish that all three tiers differ significantly in their degree of internal inequality. Micro-enterprises (Gini > 0.60) are substantially more internally unequal than either small or medium/large firms.


Theil T Decomposition: Within vs. Between Groups
The Lorenz curve compares tiers but cannot partition total inequality into within-tier and between-tier components. The Theil T index (GE(1)) supports an exact additive decomposition:
\[T = T_{\text{between}} + T_{\text{within}} = \sum_g \frac{n_g}{N}\frac{\mu_g}{\mu}\ln\frac{\mu_g}{\mu} \;+\; \sum_g \frac{n_g}{N}\frac{\mu_g}{\mu} T_g\] where \(T_g\) is the within-group Theil T for group \(g\), \(n_g\) is the group count, \(\mu_g\) is the group mean capital, and \(\mu\) is the overall mean.


Code
theil_t <- function(x) {
  x  <- x[x > 0 & !is.na(x)]
  mu <- mean(x)
  mean((x / mu) * log(x / mu))
}

decomp <- co |>
  group_by(size) |>
  summarise(
    n_g = n(),
    mu_g = mean(capital_stock),
    T_g  = theil_t(capital_stock),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    N             = sum(n_g),
    mu            = sum(n_g * mu_g) / N,
    w_g           = (n_g / N) * (mu_g / mu),
    T_between_g   = w_g * log(mu_g / mu),
    T_within_g    = w_g * T_g
  )

T_between <- sum(decomp$T_between_g)
T_within  <- sum(decomp$T_within_g)
T_total   <- T_between + T_within

# Panel A: total within vs between
summary_bar <- tibble(
  component = c("Between tiers", "Within tiers"),
  value     = c(T_between, T_within),
  pct       = c(T_between / T_total, T_within / T_total)
)

p_summary <- ggplot(summary_bar, aes(x = component, y = pct, fill = component)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1),
                     name = "Share of total Theil T") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Between tiers" = "#264653", "Within tiers" = "#e76f51"),
    guide  = "none"
  ) +
  labs(title    = "Total decomposition",
       subtitle = glue("Total Theil T = {round(T_total, 3)}"),
       x = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#264653"),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor   = element_blank())

# Panel B: within-tier contributions broken down by size
p_within <- decomp |>
  mutate(pct_of_total = T_within_g / T_total) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_rev(size), y = pct_of_total, fill = size)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.55, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(pct_of_total, accuracy = 0.1)),
            vjust = -0.5, fontface = "bold", size = 4.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), limits = c(0, 0.7),
                     name = "Within-tier share of total Theil T") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_size, guide = "none") +
  labs(
    title    = "Within-tier breakdown by size",
    subtitle = "Each bar = one tier's contribution to the within-group component",
    x = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", colour = "#264653"),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor   = element_blank())

p_summary + p_within +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Most of Brazil's capital inequality is *within* size tiers, not between them",
    subtitle = glue(
      "Theil T decomposition. Within-tier component = {percent(T_within / T_total, 0.1)} ",
      "of total inequality — the largest driver is inequality inside micro-enterprises."
    ),
    caption = caption_base,
    theme   = theme(
      plot.title    = element_markdown(face = "bold", size = 14, colour = "#264653"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.5, lineheight = 1.3),
      plot.caption  = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8)
    )
  )









The decomposition is the key structural result of this section. If most inequality were between tiers, then adjusting the revenue classification cutoffs could reduce aggregate inequality. But if the dominant component is within tiers, revenue-threshold reform achieves little: the same cutoff that separates micro from small cannot distinguish capital-poor subsistence firms from well-endowed holding-company subsidiaries within the micro category. The within-tier dominance directly motivates the governance analysis that follows.

title: Member 2 Zhijun He
text: Data and Setup

title: Member 2 Zhijun He
text:
Packages


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)          # bootstrap Gini confidence intervals
library(quantreg)      # quantile regression
library(broom)         # tidy model outputs
library(gt)            # publication-quality tables
library(modelsummary)  # regression summary tables
library(ggtext)        # markdown / HTML in ggplot labels
library(glue)          # string interpolation

# ── Shared palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653")
pal_gov  <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#e9c46a",
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#4a90d9",
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#264653"
)

caption_base <- "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry ·He, Miao & Boyd"




Data Ingestion and Cleaning


Code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Core recoding ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
co <- companies |>
  # Drop records with missing or non-positive capital (not usable for log-scale analysis)
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    capital_stock > 0,
    !is.na(owner_qualification)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Size tier (ordered factor)
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    # Governance archetype: collapse owner_qualification into 4 classes
    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    # Log10 capital for distributional analysis
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Legal-form shorthand for micro analysis
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Prop.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    ),

    # Capital-rich flag: top quartile *within* micro-enterprises
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Final observations:", scales::comma(nrow(co)), "\n")


Final observations: 141,332

title: Member 2 Zhijun He
text:
Analysis II — The Governance Premium

Capital Distribution by Governance Archetype
We examine the unconditional distribution of log-capital by governance archetype within each size tier. Quantile lines at Q1, Q2, and Q3 are shown. Kruskal-Wallis tests (non-parametric, appropriate given non-normal capital distributions) evaluate whether governance predicts capital within each tier.


Code
# Kruskal-Wallis p-values per size tier
kw_pvals <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size) |>
  summarise(
    p = kruskal.test(capital_stock ~ governance)$p.value,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    p_lab = if_else(p < 0.001, "KW: p < 0.001", glue("KW: p = {round(p, 3)}"))
  )

gov_medians <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(med_cap = median(capital_stock), n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    med_lab = glue("md: {number(med_cap, scale = 1e-3, suffix = 'k', accuracy = 1)}")
  )

ggplot(
  co |> filter(governance != "Other"),
  aes(x = log_capital, y = governance, fill = governance)
) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    alpha          = 0.75, scale = 0.88,
    quantile_lines = TRUE, quantiles = c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75),
    colour = "white", linewidth = 0.35
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data        = gov_medians,
    aes(x = log10(med_cap) + 0.05, y = governance, label = med_lab),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    size = 2.8, colour = "grey20", hjust = 0, vjust = -1.5
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data        = kw_pvals,
    aes(x = Inf, y = Inf, label = p_lab),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1.1, vjust = 1.5,
    size = 3.0, colour = "grey30", fontface = "italic"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~size, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_gov, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = c(5, 5.5, 6, 6.5, 7, 7.5, 8),
    labels = c("100k", "316k", "1M", "3.2M", "10M", "32M", "100M"),
    name   = "Capital stock (BRL, log10 scale)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Beneficial Owners command higher capital at **every** size tier",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Density ridges by governance archetype within each firm-size tier.\n",
      "Vertical white lines mark Q1, median, and Q3. ",
      "KW = Kruskal-Wallis non-parametric group comparison."
    ),
    y       = NULL,
    caption = caption_base
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_markdown(face = "bold", size = 14, colour = "#264653"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.5,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 14)),
    plot.caption       = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, colour = "#264653"),
    strip.background   = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  )











Quantile Regression: Does the Governance Premium Vary Across the Distribution?
A median regression (τ = 0.5) describes the typical firm, but development policy is most concerned with the lower tail (genuinely capital-constrained entrepreneurs) and the upper tail (well-capitalized investment vehicles masquerading as micro-enterprises). Quantile regression at τ ∈ {0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90} reveals whether the governance premium is uniform or systematically concentrated at the extremes.


Code
taus <- c(0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90)

# Recode to short labels — keeps coefficient names clean
qr_data <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  mutate(
    gov = fct_recode(governance,
      "Managing Partner" = "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner" = "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)"
    ),
    gov = relevel(gov, ref = "Sole Entrepreneur")
  )

# Point estimates only — avoids singular matrix from NID SE
qr_tidy <- map_dfr(taus, \(tau) {
  fit <- rq(log_capital ~ gov + size, tau = tau, data = qr_data, method = "fn")
  tibble(term = names(coef(fit)), estimate = coef(fit), tau = tau)
}) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "^gov")) |>
  mutate(gov_label    = str_remove(term, "^gov"),
         effect_ratio = 10^estimate)

# OLS mean-effect comparison
ols_coef <- lm(log_capital ~ gov + size, data = qr_data) |>
  tidy() |>
  filter(str_detect(term, "^gov")) |>
  mutate(gov_label    = str_remove(term, "^gov"),
         effect_ratio = 10^estimate)

pal_gov_short <- c("Managing Partner" = "#4a90d9", "Beneficial Owner" = "#264653")

ggplot(qr_tidy, aes(x = tau, y = effect_ratio, colour = gov_label)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, colour = "grey60",
             linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(data = ols_coef,
             aes(yintercept = effect_ratio, colour = gov_label),
             linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.65, alpha = 0.55) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~gov_label, nrow = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = pal_gov_short, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = taus, labels = paste0("t=", taus)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = \(x) paste0(round(x, 2), "x"),
                     name   = "Capital multiplier vs. Sole Entrepreneur baseline") +
  labs(
    title    = "The governance premium **widens at the upper tail** — especially for Beneficial Owners",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Quantile regression point estimates (exponentiated) at tau = 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90.\n",
      "Baseline = Sole Entrepreneur. Dashed line = OLS conditional mean."
    ),
    x = "Quantile (tau)", caption = caption_base
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_markdown(face = "bold", size = 14, colour = "#264653"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.2,
                                    lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 14)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, colour = "#264653"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(size = 9)
  )









The quantile regression reveals a pattern OLS obscures: the governance premium is not uniform. For Beneficial Owners, the capital multiplier relative to sole entrepreneurs grows substantially from the 10th to the 90th percentile. This asymmetry is diagnostic — Beneficial Owner-controlled firms are not simply “shifted up” relative to sole entrepreneurs but are a structurally different population that saturates the upper tail of the capital distribution. The OLS estimate (dashed line) understates the premium at the upper tail and overstates it at the lower tail.

title: Member 3 Jerry Miao
text: Data and Setup

title: Member 3 Jerry Miao
text:
Packages


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)          # bootstrap Gini confidence intervals
library(quantreg)      # quantile regression
library(broom)         # tidy model outputs
library(gt)            # publication-quality tables
library(modelsummary)  # regression summary tables
library(ggtext)        # markdown / HTML in ggplot labels
library(glue)          # string interpolation

# ── Shared palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653")
pal_gov  <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#e9c46a",
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#4a90d9",
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#264653"
)

caption_base <- "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry ·He, Miao & Boyd"




Data Ingestion and Cleaning


Code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Core recoding ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
co <- companies |>
  # Drop records with missing or non-positive capital (not usable for log-scale analysis)
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    capital_stock > 0,
    !is.na(owner_qualification)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Size tier (ordered factor)
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    # Governance archetype: collapse owner_qualification into 4 classes
    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    # Log10 capital for distributional analysis
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Legal-form shorthand for micro analysis
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Prop.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    ),

    # Capital-rich flag: top quartile *within* micro-enterprises
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Final observations:", scales::comma(nrow(co)), "\n")


Final observations: 141,332

title: Member 3 Jerry Miao
section: Analysis III — The Capital-Rich Micro: Who Escapes the Trap?
text:
Analysis III — The Capital-Rich Micro: Who Escapes the Trap?

Escape Rates by Legal Form x Governance


Code
micro_profile <- co |>
  filter(size == "Micro", governance != "Other", legal_short != "Other")

escape_rate <- micro_profile |>
  group_by(legal_short, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n_total    = n(),
    n_rich     = sum(is_capital_rich_micro),
    escape_pct = n_rich / n_total,
    # Wilson score confidence interval for a proportion
    ci_lo = {
      p <- escape_pct; n <- n_total; z <- 1.96
      (p + z^2 / (2 * n) - z * sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n + z^2 / (4 * n^2))) /
        (1 + z^2 / n)
    },
    ci_hi = {
      p <- escape_pct; n <- n_total; z <- 1.96
      (p + z^2 / (2 * n) + z * sqrt(p * (1 - p) / n + z^2 / (4 * n^2))) /
        (1 + z^2 / n)
    },
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_total >= 30)

ggplot(
  escape_rate,
  aes(
    x    = fct_reorder(legal_short, escape_pct, .fun = mean),
    y    = escape_pct,
    fill = governance
  )
) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
           width = 0.65, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    width = 0.22, colour = "grey35", linewidth = 0.55
  ) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(escape_pct, accuracy = 0.1), y = ci_hi),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    hjust = -0.15, size = 2.8, colour = "grey25"
  ) +
  coord_flip(clip = "off") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    limits = c(0, 0.60),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.05)),
    name   = "Share above 75th capital percentile (Wilson 95% CI)"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal_gov, name = "Governance archetype") +
  labs(
    title    = "Beneficial Owner-controlled LLCs are most likely to escape the capital trap",
    subtitle = glue(
      "Among micro-enterprises only (N = {comma(nrow(micro_profile))}).\n",
      "Bars = share above 75th capital percentile (BRL {comma(unique(co$capital_q75_micro)[1])}).",
      " Error bars = Wilson 95% CI. Cells with N < 30 excluded."
    ),
    x       = NULL,
    caption = caption_base
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title         = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, colour = "#264653"),
    plot.subtitle      = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.5,
                                      lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 14)),
    plot.caption       = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    legend.position    = "top",
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank()
  )











Logistic Regression: Odds Ratios for Capital-Rich Status
The bar chart above shows unconditional escape rates. A logistic model with both governance archetype and legal form as predictors estimates the independent contribution of each dimension to capital-rich status, holding the other constant.


Code
logit_fit <- glm(
  is_capital_rich_micro ~ governance + legal_short,
  data = micro_profile |>
    mutate(
      governance  = relevel(factor(governance),  ref = "Sole Entrepreneur"),
      legal_short = relevel(factor(legal_short), ref = "Sole Prop.")
    ),
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

or_df <- tidy(logit_fit, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    category   = if_else(str_detect(term, "^governance"), "Governance", "Legal Form"),
    term_clean = str_remove(term, "^governance|^legal_short"),
    term_clean = str_replace_all(term_clean, "\\.", " "),
    sig_label  = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ "**",
      p.value < 0.05  ~ "*",
      TRUE            ~ ""
    ),
    or_label = glue("{round(estimate, 2)}{sig_label}")
  )

ggplot(or_df,
       aes(x = estimate,
           y = fct_reorder(term_clean, estimate),
           colour = category)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "grey60", linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high),
                 height = 0.3, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = or_label, x = conf.high),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3.2, colour = "grey25") +
  facet_wrap(~category, scales = "free_y", ncol = 2) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c("Governance" = "#264653", "Legal Form" = "#4a90d9"),
    guide  = "none"
  ) +
  scale_x_log10(
    name   = "Odds Ratio (log scale)  —  ref: Sole Entrepreneur x Sole Prop.",
    labels = \(x) round(x, 2)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Beneficial Ownership and LLC form are the strongest predictors of capital-rich micro status",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Logistic regression odds ratios with 95% profile likelihood CI.\n",
      "Outcome: micro-enterprise above 75th capital percentile.\n",
      "Reference categories: Sole Entrepreneur governance x Sole Proprietorship legal form.\n",
      "Significance stars: *** p<0.001  ** p<0.01  * p<0.05"
    ),
    y       = NULL,
    caption = caption_base
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13, colour = "#264653"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10.2,
                                    lineheight = 1.3, margin = margin(b = 14)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8, hjust = 0),
    strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, colour = "#264653"),
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey95", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

title: Member 3 Jerry Miao
section: Regression Table: Nested OLS Models of Log Capital
text:
Regression Table: Nested OLS Models of Log Capital
The logistic model above is restricted to micro-enterprises. To assess the governance premium across the full sample, we estimate four nested OLS models regressing log10 capital stock on size, governance, and their interaction. The incremental R2 from Model 1 to Model 3 quantifies how much independent explanatory power governance adds beyond size classification alone.


Code
# Recode governance to short labels before fitting so coefficient names are clean
reg_data <- co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  mutate(
    gov = fct_recode(governance,
      "Managing Partner" = "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner" = "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Sole Entrepreneur" = "Sole Entrepreneur"
    ),
    gov = relevel(gov, ref = "Sole Entrepreneur")
  )

m1 <- lm(log_capital ~ size,      data = reg_data)
m2 <- lm(log_capital ~ gov,       data = reg_data)
m3 <- lm(log_capital ~ size + gov, data = reg_data)

modelsummary(
  list("Size only" = m1, "Governance only" = m2, "Size + Governance" = m3),
  stars      = TRUE,
  coef_rename = c(
    "(Intercept)"            = "Intercept",
    "sizeSmall"              = "Size: Small",
    "sizeMedium/Large"       = "Size: Medium / Large",
    "govManaging Partner"    = "Gov: Managing Partner",
    "govBeneficial Owner"    = "Gov: Beneficial Owner"
  ),
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared", "adj.r.squared", "rmse"),
  title   = "OLS Regression of Log10 Capital Stock on Firm Size and Governance Archetype",
  notes   = list(
    "Reference: Size = Micro, Governance = Sole Entrepreneur.",
    "DV: log10(capital_stock). SEs in parentheses. * p<0.05  ** p<0.01  *** p<0.001"
  )
)




    

    

    
    
    
      
        OLS Regression of Log10 Capital Stock on Firm Size and Governance Archetype
        
              
                 
                Size only
                Governance only
                Size + Governance
              
        
        + p < 0.1, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001
Reference: Size = Micro, Governance = Sole Entrepreneur.
DV: log10(capital_stock). SEs in parentheses. * p<0.05  ** p<0.01  *** p<0.001
        
                
                  Intercept
                  5.596***

                  5.690***
                
                
                  
                  (0.002)

                  Size: Small
                  0.132***
                  
                  0.154***
                
                
                  
                  (0.004)
                  
                  (0.004)
                
                
                  Size: Medium / Large
                  0.537***
                  
                  0.562***
                
                
                  
                  (0.004)

                  -0.200***

                  Num.Obs.

The R2 increment from Model 1 (size only) to Model 3 (size + governance) reveals the independent predictive power of governance. Because size classification is the current policy instrument, this increment is a lower bound on the information gain from incorporating governance records into capital-support targeting. The interaction model (M4) tests whether the governance premium is heterogeneous across size tiers — a finding directly relevant to program design.

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Project Description
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional)(https://thebrazilbusiness.com/article/taxation-regime-simples-nacional) sorts registered companies into micro, small, and medium/large tiers based on annual gross revenue. But revenue may be a poor proxy for capital endowment. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL per year is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether they hold 10,000 BRL or 10,000,000 BRL in declared capital stock. This raises what might be called a classification paradox: the same regulatory label could encompass both subsistence operations and well-capitalized investment vehicles.
This project uses Brazil’s CNPJ business registry(Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica–National Registry of Legal Entities) (https://dados.gov.br/dados/conjuntos-dados/cadastro-nacional-da-pessoa-juridica—cnpj) — a near-census of all registered firms — to expose the internal heterogeneity that aggregate size categories conceal. We focus on two orthogonal dimensions that revenue-based classification ignores: within-tier capital inequality and governance structure (the legal role of the controlling owner). Our central question is whether “micro-enterprise” functions as a coherent economic category or a regulatory label that shelters firms with vastly different capital endowments under the same umbrella — with direct implications for how development policy should target capital support.
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Research Questions

Within-tier inequality: To what extent do capital endowments vary among micro-enterprises within the same regulatory tier? How does within-tier capital inequality, measured by the Gini coefficient, compare across firm-size classifications?
Governance as a capital predictor: How does owner qualification — Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, or Sole Entrepreneur — relate to capital accumulation independently of firm size? To what extent does a governance premium persist within the same regulatory tier?
The “capital-rich micro”: What combinations of legal form and governance archetype characterize micro-enterprises with unusually high declared capital stock? What does this pattern suggest about how development programs might better identify genuinely capital-constrained firms?
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Dataset
Brazilian Companies (CNPJ)
The dataset is drawn from Brazil’s Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica (CNPJ), the federal business registry administered by the Receita Federal (Federal Revenue Service). It contains registration records for firms across Brazil with the following key variables:
The dataset is publicly available via the TidyTuesday GitHub repository and requires no restricted access or IRB approval | Variable | Type | Description | |—|—|—| | capital_social | Numeric (BRL) | Declared capital stock at registration | | porte_empresa | Categorical | Firm size tier under Simples Nacional (Micro, Small, Medium/Large) | | natureza_juridica | Categorical | Legal form (LLC, sole proprietorship, EIRELI, etc.) | | owner_qualification | Categorical | Legal role of controlling owner (Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, Sole Entrepreneur, Other) | | municipio | Categorical | Municipality of registration | | cnae_fiscal | Categorical | Primary sector code (CNAE classification) |
The working subset used in this project contains 141,332 observations after standard cleaning (removal of records with missing or zero capital stock, harmonization of legal-form codes). Capital stock is right-skewed and will be log-transformed for distributional analysis. The owner_qualification field is recoded into four governance archetypes: Managing Partner / Administrator, Sole Entrepreneur, Beneficial Owner (passive/investor), and Other.
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Inspiration
This project grew out of a prior TidyTuesday analysis that identified a macro-level capital paradox: micro-enterprises constitute the majority of registered Brazilian firms yet control a disproportionately small share of total capital stock. That finding raises a harder question — is this gap primarily between size tiers, within them, or both simultaneously?
The answer matters for development policy. If the capital deficit is concentrated among a genuine subset of micro-enterprises while others are actually well-capitalized holding-company subsidiaries registered as “micro” because their revenue falls below the Simples Nacional threshold, then blanket micro-enterprise subsidies are a blunt and potentially misdirected instrument. This project is motivated by the broader development economics question of when categorical targeting works and when it fails — a question with relevance well beyond Brazil, extending to informal economy support programs across Sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia, and Latin America.
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

Communication Plan

Primary channel: Google group chat (backup: email)
Expected response time: Within 24 hours on weekdays; best-effort on weekends
Standing meeting: Wednesdays, 7:00–8:00 PM (location: TBD or Zoom)
Async coordination: GitHub Issues and pull request comments for all code-related decisions; brief meeting notes shared in group chat after each session
Milestone check-ins: 48-hour heads-up in group chat before any submission deadline

All team members are expected to contribute substantively to analysis, writing, and visualization. Leads exist for accountability, not sole ownership.

title: Appendix A — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Conflict Resolution
Missed internal deadlines: If a team member anticipates missing an internal deadline, they notify the group chat at least 24 hours in advance with a revised timeline. The team redistributes the task for the current milestone; the member makes up the contribution before the next deliverable. Repeated missed deadlines — defined as two or more without prior notice — are escalated to the instructor following a team conversation.
Disagreements (50/50 split): Each side presents their case for up to five minutes, then the team votes again. If still tied:

Code and methodology questions → Technical Lead (Jerry Miao) has the deciding vote.
Framing, writing, and communication questions → Point of Contact (Zhijun He) has the deciding vote.

If the disagreement is substantive enough that neither tie-breaker feels appropriate, we consult the instructor or a preceptor before the next deadline.
General norm: Raise concerns early and in the group chat — not in GitHub comments or after a milestone has been submitted.
crumbs: A  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text: Case Study: Uber Is Taking Millions Of Manhattan Rides Away From Taxis

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Source
Fischer-Baum, Reuben, and Carl Bialik. 2015.
Uber Is Taking Millions Of Manhattan Rides Away From Taxis.
FiveThirtyEight.
https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/uber-is-taking-millions-of-manhattan-rides-away-from-taxis/

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What is the data story?
The Story the article revealed is that Uber’s growth in New York City did not simply add more total rides on top of the existing taxi system. However, in Manhattan especially, Uber appears to have taken millions of rides away from taxis. The article expressed a common concern that Uber was directly increasing congestion by showing that many of Uber’s gains were matched by taxi losses.}

title: Appendix B — Case Study
section: How the report unfolds the story
text:
How the report unfolds the story
The report unfolds in a very efficient way. First, the title and subtitle state the structure of the story very clearly, so readers can understand what the article is going to talk about. Then the article gives political and policy context by explaining concerns about congestion and the debate over Uber’s impact in New York City. This leads to why this analysis matters.
Secondly, the story introduces a simple comparison of pickup counts between years 2014 and 2015. Very easy to understand concept: Uber pickups rise sharply, yellow taxi pickups fall, and the total number of pickups changes only slightly. So the demand for ride is still there. Then the first chart works as the foundation of the entire story because it introduces the idea of substitution rather than pure growth in rides.
After that, the article becomes more detailed by asking where the changes happened. The maps are important because they move the argument from a citywide summary to a geographic explanation. They show that in Manhattan’s core area, Uber gains closely match taxi losses, while in outer boroughs both services sometimes increase. This adds spatial depth to the claim and helps readers see that Uber’s role differs by location.
Then the report then uses a scatterplot to make the strongest analytical point relationship wise. By comparing the change in Uber pickups to the change in taxi pickups across Manhattan taxi zones, the visualization shows a strong negative relationship. This supports the idea that Uber was replacing taxi rides on something close to a one-to-one basis in many places.
Finally, the article includes limitations and exceptions. It explains that pickup counts do not capture the full picture of congestion because they do not include trip length, dropoffs, or all competing services. That ending is effective because it strengthens credibility. The article still makes a strong claim, but it avoids pretending the data proves more than it actually can.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What is effective?
The narrative structure of the story is very strong. It does not just present a collection of charts. Instead, it starts with a broad claim, gives context, presents simple evidence first, then gradually builds toward more detailed and convincing visual support. That makes the story very easy to follow.
Another strength is the progression of visual complexity. The report begins with a basic comparison chart, then moves to maps, and finally to a more analytical scatterplot. This sequence helps readers build understanding step by step instead of overwhelming them all at once.
The visualizations are also tightly connected to the argument. Each one answers a specific question. The early chart answers whether total pickups increased much. The maps answer where the changes happened. The scatterplot answers how closely Uber gains matched taxi losses. Because every figure has a purpose, the report feels focused and persuasive.
A final strength is that the article balances confidence with caution. It makes a strong argument, but it also acknowledges what the data cannot show. That makes the overall story more trustworthy.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
What could be improved?
One limitation is that the article want to show causation but is limited to only correlation. The evidence strongly suggests that Uber replaced taxi rides, but the report cannot fully prove that Uber alone caused the taxi decline.
Another weakness is that pickup counts are only a partial measure of congestion. Congestion depends on more than just how many rides begin. It also depends on trip length, driving patterns between fares, dropoff locations, and the number of other vehicles on the road. The article admits this, but it is still an important limitation.
The report could also be improved by including a longer time span. Comparing only 2014 and 2015 gives a useful snapshot, but a multi-year trend would make the argument even stronger and help show whether this was a short-term shift or a stable long-term pattern.
Finally, some readers could find the scatterplot harder to interpret than the earlier charts. It is analytically strong, but it requires more statistical reading skill than the simpler visuals.

title: Appendix B — Case Study
text:
Takeaway
This case study is a strong example of data storytelling because it combines a clear claim, purposeful visualizations, and a logical progression of evidence. The article does not simply show data; it guides the reader through a sequence of questions and answers. That structure makes the final conclusion persuasive: in Manhattan, Uber appears to have replaced many taxi rides rather than substantially increasing the total number of pickups. This is a useful model for building our own project narrative, where charts should appear in an order that helps readers move from basic context to deeper interpretation.

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.1 Background and Motivation
Brazil’s official firm-size classification (Simples Nacional)(https://thebrazilbusiness.com/article/taxation-regime-simples-nacional) sorts registered companies into micro, small, and medium/large tiers based on annual gross revenue. But revenue is a poor proxy for capital endowment. A sole proprietor earning 360,000 BRL per year is legally a “micro-enterprise” regardless of whether they hold 10,000 BRL or 10,000,000 BRL in declared capital stock. This classification logic creates a structural paradox: the same regulatory label simultaneously shelters subsistence operations and well-capitalized investment vehicles.
This project uses Brazil’s CNPJ(Cadastro Nacional da Pessoa Jurídica – National Registry of Legal Entities) business registry(https://dados.gov.br/dados/conjuntos-dados/cadastro-nacional-da-pessoa-juridica—cnpj) — a near-census of all registered firms — to expose the internal heterogeneity that aggregate size categories conceal. We focus on two orthogonal dimensions that revenue-based classification ignores:
Our central argument is that “micro-enterprise” is not a coherent economic category for capital analysis — and that development policy instruments calibrated to revenue thresholds alone will systematically misidentify their target population.
crumbs: C  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
Within-tier capital inequality: How unequal is the capital distribution inside each size tier, and how much of total inequality is within versus between tiers?
Governance structure: Does the legal role of the controlling owner (Managing Partner, Beneficial Owner, Sole Entrepreneur) predict capital accumulation independently of size, and does this governance premium vary across the capital distribution?
crumbs: C  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.2 Research Questions

Within-tier inequality: Are micro-enterprises internally unequal in capital endowments? How do bootstrapped Gini coefficients compare across size tiers, and what share of total inequality is attributable to between-tier versus within-tier variation?
Governance as a capital predictor: Does owner qualification predict capital accumulation independently of firm size? Is the governance premium uniform across the capital distribution, or does it widen or narrow at the tails?
The “capital-rich micro”: Among micro-enterprises above the 75th capital percentile, what legal form-governance combinations most strongly predict capital-rich status? What are the odds ratios from a logistic model of capital-rich membership?
crumbs: C  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.3 Data and Setup

Packages


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(patchwork)
library(boot)          # bootstrap Gini confidence intervals
library(quantreg)      # quantile regression
library(broom)         # tidy model outputs
library(gt)            # publication-quality tables
library(modelsummary)  # regression summary tables
library(ggtext)        # markdown / HTML in ggplot labels
library(glue)          # string interpolation

# ── Shared palette ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
pal_size <- c("Micro" = "#e76f51", "Small" = "#f4a261", "Medium/Large" = "#264653")
pal_gov  <- c(
  "Sole Entrepreneur"                    = "#e9c46a",
  "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator"    = "#4a90d9",
  "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)" = "#264653"
)

caption_base <- "Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry ·He, Miao & Boyd"




Data Ingestion and Cleaning


Code
companies <- read_csv(
  "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2026/2026-01-27/companies.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

# ── Core recoding ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
co <- companies |>
  # Drop records with missing or non-positive capital (not usable for log-scale analysis)
  filter(
    !is.na(company_size),
    !is.na(capital_stock),
    capital_stock > 0,
    !is.na(owner_qualification)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Size tier (ordered factor)
    size = case_when(
      company_size == "micro-enterprise" ~ "Micro",
      company_size == "small-enterprise" ~ "Small",
      TRUE                               ~ "Medium/Large"
    ),
    size = factor(size, levels = c("Micro", "Small", "Medium/Large")),

    # Governance archetype: collapse owner_qualification into 4 classes
    governance = case_when(
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("beneficial owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("entrepreneur|business owner", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Sole Entrepreneur",
      str_detect(owner_qualification,
                 regex("managing partner|administrator", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~
        "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    governance = factor(governance, levels = c(
      "Sole Entrepreneur",
      "Managing Partner /\nAdministrator",
      "Beneficial Owner\n(passive/investor)",
      "Other"
    )),

    # Log10 capital for distributional analysis
    log_capital = log10(capital_stock),

    # Legal-form shorthand for micro analysis
    legal_short = case_when(
      str_detect(legal_nature, "LLC")                 ~ "LLC",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Sole Proprietorship") ~ "Sole Prop.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Individual Limited")  ~ "EIRELI",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Simple")              ~ "Simple Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Silent")              ~ "Silent Part.",
      str_detect(legal_nature, "Corporation")         ~ "Corporation",
      TRUE                                            ~ "Other"
    ),

    # Capital-rich flag: top quartile *within* micro-enterprises
    capital_q75_micro     = quantile(capital_stock[size == "Micro"], 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_capital_rich_micro = (size == "Micro") & (capital_stock >= capital_q75_micro)
  )

cat("Final observations:", scales::comma(nrow(co)), "\n")


Final observations: 141,332 




Descriptive Summary Table


Code
co |>
  filter(governance != "Other") |>
  group_by(size, governance) |>
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    med_cap  = median(capital_stock),
    mean_cap = mean(capital_stock),
    p25_cap  = quantile(capital_stock, 0.25),
    p75_cap  = quantile(capital_stock, 0.75),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) |>
  gt(groupname_col = "size") |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Capital Stock by Firm Size and Governance Archetype**"),
    subtitle = "All values in BRL · Median, mean, and interquartile range"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    governance = "Governance Archetype",
    n          = "N",
    med_cap    = "Median",
    mean_cap   = "Mean",
    p25_cap    = "P25",
    p75_cap    = "P75"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = c(med_cap, mean_cap, p25_cap, p75_cap),
    suffixing = TRUE, decimals = 1
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = n, use_seps = TRUE, decimals = 0) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size            = px(13),
    heading.align              = "left",
    row_group.background.color = "#f4f4f4"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(md("*Source: Brazilian CNPJ Registry*"))

crumbs: C  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil

title: Appendix C — Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
text:
C.4 Synthesis
This analysis reframes the Brazilian micro-enterprise capital paradox across four dimensions.
1. The trap operates at two scales simultaneously. The Theil T decomposition shows that a majority of total capital inequality is within size tiers, not between them. Revenue-based classification reform cannot close the capital gap, because the gap is not primarily a between-tier phenomenon.
2. Governance is an independent, orthogonal capital predictor. The nested OLS models confirm that incorporating governance structure adds statistically significant explanatory power beyond size alone. The governance premium is not a proxy for size.
3. The premium is not uniform: it widens at the upper tail. Quantile regression at τ ∈ {0.10, …, 0.90} shows that the Beneficial Owner advantage over sole entrepreneurs grows substantially at higher quantiles. OLS estimates the conditional mean, which masks this distributional asymmetry entirely. The upper tail of the micro-enterprise capital distribution is disproportionately occupied by Beneficial Owner-controlled firms resembling holding-company subsidiaries, not organic small businesses.
4. Capital-rich micro status is predictable from governance and legal form. The logistic model confirms that Beneficial Ownership and LLC legal form are the strongest independent predictors of capital-rich status within the micro tier, with odds ratios significantly above 1 after controlling for both dimensions simultaneously. Sole Entrepreneurs in Sole Proprietorships — the most structurally exposed population — have the lowest probability of capital-rich status and the highest probability of genuine capital constraint.
Policy implication. If Brazil’s development goal is to direct capital support toward genuinely constrained small entrepreneurs, the targeting instrument should integrate governance structure alongside revenue-based size classification. The owner_qualification field is already present in CNPJ administrative records at zero additional data collection cost. Adding it as a screening variable would substantially improve the precision of capital-support programs. Revenue thresholds are necessary but insufficient — and this dataset shows precisely why.
crumbs: C  Capital Without Category: Governance, Inequality, and the Micro-Enterprise Paradox in Brazil
